title: Severe Preeclampsia with HELLP Syndrome in 32-Year-Old G1P0 at 34 Weeks + 2 Days: Magnesium Sulfate Seizure Prophylaxis with DTR + Respiratory + UOP Toxicity Surveillance + Calcium Gluconate Antidote, Severe-Range Blood Pressure Treatment within 60 Minutes, Antenatal Betamethasone for Fetal Lung Maturity, Delivery Planning, and Postpartum Eclampsia Vigilance × 24–48 Hours
subtitle: SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA diagnostic criteria per ACOG 2020 = preeclampsia (new HTN ≥140/90 + proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction at ≥20 wk in previously normotensive woman) PLUS ≥1 severe feature (BP ≥160/110 sustained × ≥4 hr or treated; PLATELETS <100k; AST/ALT ≥2× ULN; new HEPATIC pain unresponsive to medication; SERUM Cr ≥1.1 or doubled; PULMONARY EDEMA; new visual or cerebral symptoms); HELLP syndrome = HEMOLYSIS (schistocytes + LDH ≥600 OR T bili ≥1.2 OR haptoglobin <30) + ELEVATED LIVER enzymes (AST or ALT ≥2× ULN; often >70 IU/L) + LOW PLATELETS (<100k); MISSISSIPPI classification (class 1 platelets ≤50k = worst; class 2 50–100k; class 3 100–150k); TENNESSEE classification (complete vs incomplete); MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4–6 g IV loading dose over 15–20 min then 1–2 g/hr maintenance infusion for SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS + neuroprotection — TOXICITY MONITORING q1h: DEEP TENDON REFLEXES (loss = early toxicity at Mg 10), RESPIRATORY RATE (<12/min = severe toxicity at Mg 12–14), URINE OUTPUT (<30 mL/hr = renal accumulation risk; magnesium renally cleared), MENTAL STATUS, magnesium levels (therapeutic 4–7 mEq/L; loss of patellar reflex at 8–10; respiratory depression 10–13; cardiac arrest >25); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV SLOW over 3 minutes is the ANTIDOTE for magnesium toxicity — keep available at bedside; HOLD magnesium for ANY toxicity sign + administer calcium gluconate + monitor + reassess + restart at lower rate if appropriate; SEVERE-RANGE BP (≥160/110 sustained) treated WITHIN 60 MINUTES per ACOG to prevent stroke + abruption; IV LABETALOL 20 mg → 40 → 80 mg q10 min (max cumulative 220 mg) OR IV HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg q20 min OR PO NIFEDIPINE 10 mg q20 min (no longer requires order to delay nifedipine if IV access not available); AVOID ACE-I/ARB in pregnancy (teratogenic); ANTENATAL BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hr apart for fetal lung maturity (24–34 wk; consider rescue at 24–34 wk if delivery imminent + last dose >14 days ago); DELIVERY is the only definitive treatment — at <34 wk = balance maternal-fetal risk (steroids + delivery at maternal stabilization OR 48 hr later for steroid effect IF maternal-fetal stable); at ≥34 wk = deliver after stabilization; CESAREAN vs vaginal depends on clinical status (no absolute indication for cesarean in HELLP alone but unfavorable cervix + maternal deterioration may prompt cesarean); POSTPARTUM eclampsia risk × 24–48 hr (up to 6 weeks) — continue magnesium × 24 hr postpartum + continued BP monitoring + visit at 7–10 days + 6 weeks; HELLP DIFFERENTIAL = acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP — hypoglycemia + low fibrinogen + elevated ammonia) + thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP — neurologic + pentad + ADAMTS13 <10% + plasma exchange) + atypical hemolytic uremic syndrome (aHUS — predominantly renal + complement dysregulation + eculizumab); FETAL surveillance = NST + BPP + continuous EFM during management; magnesium reduces fetal HR variability (expected) — do not mistake for fetal compromise

labs.v: 32 y/o G1P0 woman K. Nguyen at 34 wk + 2 d gestational age (confirmed by 9-wk dating ultrasound) presenting with new-onset severe HA × 12 hr (worst of life + bilateral throbbing + unresponsive to acetaminophen), RUQ + epigastric pain × 6 hr, visual blurring + photopsia × 4 hr, 3.5 kg weight gain / 5 days (suggesting new edema), and severe-range BP 168/108 + 174/112 + 172/110 across 3 readings at OB clinic at 1045. PMH: no chronic HTN, no diabetes, no autoimmune, no renal disease, no thyroid disease; pregnancy uncomplicated until 33 wk visit (BP 132/84 at last visit 1 wk ago = upper end of normal but not severe); had blood pressure rise pattern over past 2 weeks. FAMILY HISTORY: mother had preeclampsia in her first pregnancy (genetic predisposition); maternal grandmother had eclampsia (seizures postpartum). PARTNER: spouse age 34, supportive + at bedside; works as software engineer; healthy. PRENATAL CARE complete + routine; aspirin 81 mg daily for preeclampsia prevention WAS NOT initiated at 12 wk despite family history risk factor (per ACOG/USPSTF Class A recommendation for high-risk women — gap in care; aspirin reduces preeclampsia risk ~24% in moderate-high risk per ASPRE trial; will document for QI review). ON ADMISSION L&D triage: BP severe-range sustained; signs of severe preeclampsia (severe HA + RUQ pain + visual changes); LABS sent — Hgb 9.8 (down from 12.4 at 32 wk visit = hemolysis), platelets 78,000 (LOW — HELLP criterion <100k), AST 145 + ALT 168 (HELLP criterion ≥2× ULN ~40 IU/L), LDH 720 (≥600 = hemolysis criterion), T bili 1.6 (≥1.2 supports hemolysis), haptoglobin <30 (hemolysis), schistocytes on peripheral smear (hemolysis), Cr 1.0 (normal — distinguishes from severe preeclampsia with renal failure or AFLP), UA 4+ protein + UPCR 1.8 (significantly proteinuric), PT/PTT normal + fibrinogen 380 (preserved — distinguishes from DIC + AFLP which both deplete fibrinogen), glucose 92 (normal — distinguishes from AFLP hypoglycemia), ammonia 36 (normal — distinguishes from AFLP); HELLP SYNDROME CONFIRMED (Mississippi class 2 = platelets 50–100k) with all 3 criteria present = COMPLETE HELLP (not partial/incomplete). REFLEXES 3+ bilaterally without clonus; no SOB or pulmonary edema; no abdominal distension or ascites. FHR 142 baseline reactive on NST. CERVIX closed + posterior + firm + 0 station. EFW 2200 g per recent growth scan (50th percentile — appropriate for 34 wk).
labs.r: SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA + HELLP syndrome confirmed by ACOG 2020 + multi-organ criteria + laboratory findings + clinical features. HELLP is severe-preeclampsia spectrum with hematologic + hepatic involvement. Mississippi classification useful for stratification (class 1 = platelets ≤50k = worst; class 2 = 50–100k; class 3 = 100–150k). COMPLETE HELLP (all 3 criteria) vs incomplete/partial (1–2 criteria). HEMOLYSIS is the diagnostic + monitoring hallmark — LDH elevation + schistocytes + low haptoglobin + indirect bilirubin elevation. ELEVATED LIVER ENZYMES — AST/ALT ≥2× ULN. LOW PLATELETS — <100k. Severity guides delivery + critical care decisions.

labs.n: MAGNESIUM SULFATE — pharmacology + administration + toxicity surveillance + antidote
labs.v: MAGNESIUM SULFATE indication for SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA + ECLAMPSIA: SEIZURE PROPHYLAXIS (reduces eclamptic seizure incidence by ~50% per Magpie trial 2002) + NEUROPROTECTION (also for fetus < 32 wk per BEAM/PreMag trials). DOSING regimen most commonly used in US: LOADING DOSE 4–6 g IV infused over 15–20 minutes (NEVER bolus IV push); MAINTENANCE 1–2 g/hr continuous IV infusion. ROUTE: dedicated IV pump (smart pump with hard limits programmed); NEVER given with calcium-containing IV lines (precipitates); compatible with most antihypertensives in y-site. MECHANISM: NMDA receptor antagonist + smooth muscle relaxant + vasodilation + decreased uterine activity (tocolytic effect — also indication for tocolysis in some protocols). MONITORING q1h (more frequent if loading or any concern): (1) DEEP TENDON REFLEXES — patellar (preferred — easier to interpret) or biceps; 2+ normal; LOSS of patellar reflex = early sign of toxicity (Mg level 8–10 mEq/L); (2) RESPIRATORY RATE — count for full 60 sec; <12/min = SEVERE toxicity (Mg level 10–13 mEq/L); (3) URINE OUTPUT — magnesium is RENALLY CLEARED so RENAL dysfunction → accumulation → toxicity; UOP <30 mL/hr → reduce or hold mag (consider 25 mL/hr cutoff); (4) MENTAL STATUS — drowsiness, confusion, slurred speech = toxicity; (5) MAGNESIUM LEVEL drawn 4–6 hr after loading + then q6h; THERAPEUTIC level = 4–7 mEq/L (4.8–8.4 mg/dL); 8–10 = loss of patellar reflex; 10–13 = respiratory depression; 13–20 = cardiac dysfunction; >25 = cardiac arrest. ANTIDOTE = CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 gram (10 mL of 10% solution) IV slow over 3 minutes — competes with magnesium at NMJ + receptor sites; KEEP at BEDSIDE in clearly labeled med drawer for immediate access; institutional protocol = co-RN cross-check + barcode scan. IF TOXICITY OBSERVED: HOLD magnesium (turn off pump) + administer calcium gluconate + assess airway/breathing + monitor BP + cardiac rhythm + call MFM/OB hospitalist + may resume magnesium at lower rate after recovery + repeat level + clinical assessment. ADDITIONAL safety: MAGNESIUM POTENTIATES NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKADE (anesthesiology aware if cesarean — increased succinylcholine + nondepolarizing NMB effect); MAGNESIUM crosses placenta → REDUCED fetal HR variability EXPECTED (do NOT mistake for fetal compromise); HYPOTONIA + RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION in NEONATE possible (NICU prepared; resuscitation team at delivery). CONTRAINDICATIONS = myasthenia gravis (paradoxical worsening) + heart block + active hemorrhage + maternal pulmonary edema (caution). POSTPARTUM continuation × 24 hr (eclampsia risk persists postpartum; can extend if persistent severe sx).
labs.r: MAGNESIUM SULFATE pharmacology + administration + toxicity surveillance + calcium gluconate antidote are foundational to severe preeclampsia/HELLP care + a HIGH-YIELD NCLEX Reduction of Risk Potential topic. KEY POINTS: (1) 4–6 g loading over 15–20 min + 1–2 g/hr maintenance, (2) Q1H monitoring DTR + RR + UOP + mental status + magnesium level, (3) toxicity signs = loss of DTR (early) + RR <12 (severe) + UOP <30 mL/hr + altered mental status, (4) CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slow over 3 min = ANTIDOTE — keep at bedside, (5) magnesium reduces fetal HR variability — expected not concerning, (6) potentiates NMB during cesarean, (7) postpartum × 24 hr continuation. RN role: administer with high-alert med protocols + barcode scanning + dedicated pump + q1h monitoring + escalation.

labs.n: SEVERE-RANGE BP treatment — IV antihypertensives within 60 minutes
labs.v: SEVERE-RANGE BP = SBP ≥160 OR DBP ≥110 sustained for ≥15 min OR treated to prevent sustained — TREAT within 60 minutes of identification per ACOG 2020 to prevent stroke (most catastrophic complication of preeclampsia + leading cause of maternal mortality) + placental abruption + acute heart failure + pulmonary edema. FIRST-LINE OPTIONS: (1) IV LABETALOL — start 20 mg IV → repeat 40 mg at 10 min if still severe → repeat 80 mg at 10 min → continue 80 mg q10 min as needed (max cumulative 220 mg per single hypertensive crisis episode); can also use continuous infusion 1–2 mg/min; CONTRAINDICATIONS = asthma (relative), heart block, decompensated heart failure, hypersensitivity to beta-blockers; cardioselective alternative = metoprolol but less data; (2) IV HYDRALAZINE — start 5–10 mg IV → repeat 5–10 mg at 20 min if still severe (max 30 mg); produces reflex tachycardia (less ideal for tachycardic patient); avoid in coronary artery disease; (3) PO IMMEDIATE-RELEASE NIFEDIPINE — start 10 mg PO → repeat 20 mg at 20 min if still severe → repeat 20 mg at 20 min (max 50 mg in 1 hr); useful when IV access not available; not sublingual (avoid sublingual nifedipine — hypotensive risk + abruption risk); CONTRAINDICATIONS = significant aortic stenosis. AVOID in pregnancy: ACE inhibitors + ARBs (teratogenic — renal/cranial anomalies; fetal demise late pregnancy); ATENOLOL (associated with intrauterine growth restriction with chronic use); sodium nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity in mother + fetus; only short courses if absolutely needed); thiazide diuretics (volume depletion in already-volume-depleted preeclamptic patient). TARGET = SBP <160 + DBP <110 (NOT <140/90 acutely — too rapid drop risks ischemia + placental hypoperfusion); maintain gentle BP reduction over hours; continued surveillance + repeat dosing as needed. POSTPARTUM continuation of antihypertensives + outpatient transition (long-acting agents — labetalol, nifedipine ER, methyldopa — once stable + transitioning home). PREECLAMPSIA WITH SEVERE FEATURES + treated BP can convert to normotensive postpartum within days to weeks; some women develop chronic HTN. THIS PATIENT: labetalol 20 mg IV at 1200 → BP 152/98 at 1215 (improved); labetalol 40 mg at 1430 → BP 138/86 at 1445 (improved further); transitioned to oral labetalol 200 mg BID + nifedipine ER 30 mg daily after stable × 12 hr; will continue postpartum + outpatient.
labs.r: SEVERE-RANGE BP requires URGENT TREATMENT within 60 minutes per ACOG to prevent stroke + abruption + acute heart failure. IV labetalol + IV hydralazine + PO IR nifedipine are first-line. AVOID ACE-I/ARB + atenolol + nitroprusside + diuretics in pregnancy. Target SBP <160 + DBP <110 (NOT <140/90 acutely). Postpartum continuation + transition. RN role: rapid recognition + IV antihypertensive administration + repeat as needed + monitoring + escalation.

labs.v: BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hours apart for FETAL LUNG MATURITY in anticipated preterm delivery between 24+0 and 33+6 weeks (with extended administration to 34+6 if 24+0 to 33+6 not received and likely to deliver — late preterm rescue per Antenatal Late Preterm Steroid trial 2016); reduces neonatal RDS (respiratory distress syndrome) by ~40%, intraventricular hemorrhage, necrotizing enterocolitis, neonatal mortality; ALTERNATIVE = DEXAMETHASONE 6 mg IM q12h × 4 doses (equivalent efficacy in most trials); ONSET 24 hr after first dose; PEAK effect at 48 hr; OPTIMAL window: complete course (2 doses 24 hr apart) with delivery 24 hr to 7 days after second dose. RESCUE dose at 24–34 wk if last course >14 days ago + likely delivery within 7 days. MULTIPLE COURSES (more than 2) NOT recommended due to potential neurodevelopmental concerns + reduced birth weight. THIS PATIENT at 34 wk + 2 d eligible for late preterm rescue + standard antenatal corticosteroid course (eligible for 34+0 to 36+6 if NOT received between 24–33+6 AND likely to deliver within 7 days + has not received corticosteroids in current pregnancy). FIRST DOSE administered 1230 Day 0; SECOND DOSE scheduled 1230 next day (24 hr later); maternal hyperglycemia anticipated (steroid effect — monitor glucose for need of insulin sliding scale if elevated); maternal infection risk (slight, mostly in long courses); fetal HR effects transient. DELIVERY TIMING: ideally 24 hr after second dose for maximum lung maturity effect IF MATERNAL-FETAL STATUS PERMITS — if maternal deterioration or fetal compromise → delivery NOW regardless. THIS PATIENT plan: stabilize × 24 hr post-second betamethasone (target induction ~Day 2 evening or Day 3 morning IF stable + reassuring fetal status); if maternal HELLP worsens or fetal compromise → expedited delivery; cesarean vs vaginal favored vaginal initially given cervix amenable + EFW 2200 g + stable. POSTPARTUM neonate to NICU level II/III depending on respiratory status; expect mild RDS managed with CPAP + surfactant if needed + caffeine for apnea of prematurity.
labs.r: ANTENATAL BETAMETHASONE reduces neonatal RDS + IVH + NEC + mortality in preterm delivery 24–33+6 wk + rescue or late-preterm 34+ if eligible. 2 doses 24 hr apart IM. Optimal window 24 hr to 7 days post-second dose. Multiple courses not recommended. RN role: administer + document timing + coordinate with delivery planning + maternal glucose monitoring + neonate prep.

labs.n: DELIVERY PLAN — timing + route + maternal-fetal balance
labs.v: DELIVERY is the ONLY DEFINITIVE TREATMENT for preeclampsia + HELLP. TIMING decisions based on gestational age + maternal status + fetal status + steroid window + cervical status: (1) <24 wk = generally pregnancy termination considered (not viable; severe preeclampsia at extreme prematurity has poor maternal-fetal outcomes); (2) 24–34 wk = if STABLE + reassuring fetal status → expectant management with corticosteroids + maternal stabilization + delivery 48 hr post-steroid (or sooner if maternal deterioration) + MFM consultation; if unstable maternal or fetal → expedited delivery; (3) ≥34 wk = delivery after stabilization is the standard (HYPITAT trial supports delivery >34 wk for severe preeclampsia); (4) HELLP specifically = delivery generally recommended at all gestational ages because of high maternal morbidity + mortality with continued pregnancy (though 24–34 wk may still pursue 24–48 hr steroid window if maternal-fetal stable); (5) ECLAMPSIA = stabilize → deliver after seizure controlled + maternal stable. ROUTE = vaginal vs cesarean based on clinical factors: NO ABSOLUTE indication for cesarean in HELLP/severe preeclampsia alone — vaginal delivery feasible with induction + epidural; cesarean indicated for: unfavorable cervix + maternal-fetal deterioration not allowing time for induction + obstetric indications (malpresentation, prior cesarean, placenta previa, fetal distress not responsive to maternal resuscitation). ANESTHESIA: epidural preferred over general (avoids airway management risks + maternal hemodynamic instability + neonatal effects); CONTRAINDICATIONS to neuraxial = severe thrombocytopenia (platelets <70k generally considered cutoff — institutional varies; this patient at 78k borderline + may correct with platelet transfusion if cesarean needed) + active coagulopathy + sepsis at site + patient refusal. THIS PATIENT plan: STABLE on magnesium + IV antihypertensives; HELLP labs improving slightly with monitoring; cervix unfavorable; betamethasone window approaching; PLAN = expectant 24 hr post-second betamethasone IF maternal-fetal stable → induction with foley + oxytocin (cervical ripening); cesarean if maternal-fetal deterioration; epidural anesthesia (platelet 78k discussion with anesthesia — may need correction with platelets pre-procedure if cesarean); 2 large-bore IVs maintained; type & crossmatch active; OR + NICU + MFM + anesthesia + OB hospitalist + neonatology coordinated. INTRAOPERATIVE considerations if cesarean: magnesium continued through delivery (eclampsia prophylaxis); ICU-level monitoring postoperative × 24 hr; uterotonics (oxytocin first-line, methylergonovine + carboprost + misoprostol second-line — METHYLERGONOVINE contraindicated in HTN/preeclampsia + CARBOPROST contraindicated in asthma); blood products available.
labs.r: DELIVERY is the only definitive treatment for preeclampsia/HELLP. Timing depends on gestational age + maternal status + fetal status + steroid window. ≥34 wk = delivery after stabilization standard. HELLP = delivery generally recommended at all GA. Vaginal vs cesarean based on clinical factors. Epidural anesthesia preferred but consider platelet count. RN role: coordinate multidisciplinary team + monitoring + medication administration + family communication + neonatal prep.

labs.v: POSTPARTUM PREECLAMPSIA + ECLAMPSIA risk persists × 24–48 hr (highest risk) + up to 6 weeks postpartum (lower risk). MAGNESIUM SULFATE continued × 24 hr postpartum routinely + extended if persistent severe sx; can extend × 48 hr in highest-risk patients. BP MONITORING: q1h × first 24 hr + then q2–4h × 24 hr + then q4–6h until stable; OUTPATIENT follow-up: BP check 3–7 days postpartum + BP check at 7–10 days postpartum + comprehensive postpartum visit at 6 weeks. POSTPARTUM PREECLAMPSIA presenting late (typically 48 hr–6 weeks postpartum) — patient may present from home with new-onset HA + visual changes + RUQ pain + edema + HTN; require urgent reassessment + treatment + readmission. NEW-ONSET POSTPARTUM HYPERTENSION (no prior preeclampsia) — separate diagnosis + similar management. POSTPARTUM HELLP recovery: platelets typically nadir at 24–72 hr postpartum then recover; LFTs improve over days–weeks; LDH improves over days; hemolysis resolves over days; FATIGUE persistent for weeks. POSTPARTUM ANTIHYPERTENSIVES continued + transitioned (labetalol, nifedipine ER, methyldopa, hydralazine for outpatient); avoid ACE-I/ARB while breastfeeding (controversial — some are compatible with lactation per LactMed — captopril, enalapril compatible; lisinopril limited data); thiazide diuretics may reduce milk supply. POSTPARTUM EDUCATION + safety: signs of postpartum preeclampsia/eclampsia (HA, visual changes, RUQ pain, edema, SOB) — return to ER immediately; BP monitoring at home if HTN persisting; mood + emotional health support (preeclampsia survivors have higher PPD/anxiety risk); CARDIOVASCULAR RISK long-term: women with preeclampsia have 2–4× higher lifetime risk of cardiovascular disease + HTN + diabetes — counsel on lifestyle + screening + follow-up with PCP; ASPIRIN 81 mg daily prophylaxis in SUBSEQUENT PREGNANCIES (start 12–16 wk through delivery) per ACOG/USPSTF for women with prior preeclampsia (Class A); RECURRENCE risk ~15–25% in next pregnancy (higher if early-onset, severe features, or HELLP in index pregnancy). BREASTFEEDING with HELLP — encouraged + supported; magnesium during postpartum continuation is compatible with breastfeeding; antihypertensives generally compatible (labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa); lactation consultant; pump if neonate in NICU; skin-to-skin when feasible.
labs.r: POSTPARTUM ECLAMPSIA risk persists × 24–48 hr + up to 6 weeks. Magnesium × 24 hr postpartum. BP monitoring + outpatient follow-up. Postpartum preeclampsia can present late from home. HELLP recovery over days–weeks. Long-term cardiovascular risk + aspirin prophylaxis in next pregnancy + recurrence risk. Breastfeeding supported. RN role: continued monitoring + medication management + education + follow-up coordination + family support.

labs.n: DIFFERENTIAL — HELLP vs AFLP vs TTP vs aHUS
labs.v: DIFFERENTIAL diagnosis of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia in pregnancy is critical because management differs: (1) HELLP — HEMOLYSIS + ELEVATED LIVER + LOW PLATELETS; severe preeclampsia spectrum; THIRD TRIMESTER most common; treatment = delivery + magnesium + antihypertensives + supportive; PT/PTT normal (distinguishing); fibrinogen preserved (distinguishing); glucose normal (distinguishing). (2) AFLP (Acute Fatty Liver of Pregnancy) — THIRD TRIMESTER; clinical features = N/V + abdominal pain + jaundice + altered mental status + coagulopathy; LABS = HYPOGLYCEMIA + LOW FIBRINOGEN + ELEVATED AMMONIA + AST/ALT elevated (often higher than HELLP — 500–1000) + bilirubin elevated + INR elevated + Cr elevated + uric acid elevated; FATTY INFILTRATION on imaging; Swansea criteria diagnosis; TREATMENT = DELIVERY + supportive (fresh frozen plasma + cryo + glucose + plasmapheresis + N-acetylcysteine + ICU); MORTALITY higher than HELLP. (3) TTP (Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura) — can occur ANY TRIMESTER + POSTPARTUM; PENTAD = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + NEUROLOGIC signs + FEVER + RENAL dysfunction (full pentad rare; first 2 + 1 of last 3 typical); ADAMTS13 activity <10% (deficiency or autoantibody) = diagnostic; TREATMENT = URGENT PLASMA EXCHANGE + corticosteroids + rituximab + caplacizumab (anti-vWF antibody); DO NOT TRANSFUSE PLATELETS (can worsen disease — uses up vWF cleavage substrate); delivery may worsen TTP. (4) aHUS (Atypical Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome) — complement dysregulation; pregnancy-triggered in some cases; RENAL dysfunction PREDOMINATES + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia; ADAMTS13 normal; TREATMENT = ECULIZUMAB + supportive renal; delivery may not improve. KEY DISTINCTIONS: HELLP = preserved fibrinogen + normal coags + normal ammonia + normal glucose + multi-organ involvement with hepatic predominance; AFLP = HYPOGLYCEMIA + LOW fibrinogen + elevated ammonia; TTP = neurologic + fever + renal + ADAMTS13 <10%; aHUS = renal predominance + ADAMTS13 normal + complement. SHARED workup: peripheral smear + LDH + haptoglobin + ADAMTS13 + complement + coags + glucose + ammonia + LFTs + Cr + UA + electrolytes + INR + uric acid + lipase + imaging. THIS PATIENT consistent with HELLP — coags normal + fibrinogen preserved + glucose normal + ammonia normal + RUQ pain + classic preeclampsia features + hepatic + hematologic involvement.
labs.r: DIFFERENTIAL of MAHA + thrombocytopenia in pregnancy is critical. KEY POINTS: (1) HELLP = preserved coags + normal ammonia + normal glucose, (2) AFLP = HYPOGLYCEMIA + low fibrinogen + elevated ammonia, (3) TTP = neurologic + fever + ADAMTS13 <10% + plasma exchange (NOT platelets), (4) aHUS = renal + ADAMTS13 normal + eculizumab. Workup includes ADAMTS13 + complement + coags + glucose + ammonia + peripheral smear. Management differs significantly — wrong diagnosis = wrong treatment.

labs.n: ASPIRIN prophylaxis + risk factors for preeclampsia + recurrence
labs.v: PREECLAMPSIA RISK FACTORS — HIGH RISK (ACOG criteria; one of these = aspirin 81 mg recommendation): prior preeclampsia, multifetal gestation, chronic HTN, type 1 or 2 diabetes, kidney disease, autoimmune disease (lupus, antiphospholipid syndrome); MODERATE RISK (multiple of these = aspirin recommended): primigravida, age ≥35, family history preeclampsia, Black race, low socioeconomic status, BMI ≥30, prior adverse pregnancy outcome, IVF conception. ASPIRIN 81 mg DAILY starting at 12–16 weeks (after first-trimester anatomy scan + neural tube closure) through 36 weeks or delivery — per ACOG + USPSTF Class A recommendation; reduces preeclampsia incidence by 24% (per ASPRE trial 2017 — high-risk women) + reduces preterm preeclampsia by ~50%. THIS PATIENT had family history of preeclampsia (mother + maternal grandmother) + primigravida + age 32 = MODERATE risk profile per ACOG — ASPIRIN 81 mg WAS NOT initiated at 12 wk (gap in care; will document for QI review). PREECLAMPSIA RECURRENCE risk in subsequent pregnancy: ~15–25% baseline if prior preeclampsia; HIGHER if early-onset (<34 wk) or severe features or HELLP — up to 35–55% recurrence; recurrence often EARLIER + more severe in subsequent pregnancy. PROPHYLAXIS in subsequent pregnancy = aspirin 81 mg starting 12–16 wk + early surveillance + MFM consultation + low-dose calcium supplementation if low-dietary calcium baseline (some evidence for benefit). LONG-TERM CARDIOVASCULAR RISK: women with preeclampsia have 2–4× higher lifetime cardiovascular disease risk + 2–3× higher HTN risk + 2× higher diabetes risk + 1.5–2× higher stroke risk; counsel on lifestyle + screening + follow-up with PCP — CARDIAC RISK assessment 6–12 months postpartum + annually; lifestyle = healthy diet + physical activity + weight management + tobacco cessation + Mediterranean diet style; SCREENINGS = BP + lipid + glucose + UMACR + BMI + waist circumference.
labs.r: ASPIRIN 81 mg prophylaxis 12–16 wk through delivery reduces preeclampsia 24% in high-risk women per ACOG/USPSTF Class A. THIS PATIENT had moderate risk profile (family hx + primigravida + age) + aspirin gap. RECURRENCE risk in next pregnancy 15–25% baseline + higher if early-onset/severe/HELLP. LONG-TERM cardiovascular risk + lifestyle + screening counsel. RN role: identify risk factors + educate + advocate for prophylaxis + ensure follow-up.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES: no seizures (eclampsia not yet — magnesium prophylaxis aim); no pulmonary edema (lungs clear; UOP adequate; fluid restriction 80 mL/hr maintained); no DIC (PT/PTT normal + fibrinogen preserved); no AFLP features (glucose normal + ammonia normal + fibrinogen preserved); no TTP features (no neurologic + no fever + ADAMTS13 not yet sent but unlikely given preserved fibrinogen + RUQ pain + classic preeclampsia presentation); no aHUS features (renal function normal); no placental abruption (no bleeding + fetal HR reassuring); no fetal distress (variability reduced expected on magnesium; reactive NSTs limited but no decelerations); no maternal heart failure; no stroke; no chronic HTN or diabetes prior to pregnancy; supportive family + spouse engaged; insurance coverage active; access to multidisciplinary team (MFM + OB hospitalist + anesthesia + NICU + perinatology + lactation + social work + chaplain); patient + family receptive + cooperative + understanding goals of care + family planning aware; vegetable + protein-rich diet; nonsmoker + alcohol-free. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: well-resourced + accessing care + receptive to multidisciplinary team + adequate facility (level III with NICU); supportive spouse + maternal grandmother at bedside; magnesium toxicity surveillance per protocol; calcium gluconate at bedside; betamethasone window protective for neonate; cervix amenable to ripening (avoiding cesarean); HELLP labs improving slightly with treatment (will continue to trend); MFM + neonatology + anesthesia + OB hospitalist multidisciplinary engagement; fetal monitoring continuous; healthy gestational age 34 wk + EFW appropriate; no comorbidities; engaged + cooperative + literate + family-supported.
labs.r: Multiple PROTECTIVE FACTORS support good maternal-neonatal outcomes despite serious diagnosis: well-resourced + receptive + supportive family + level III facility + multidisciplinary team + protocol adherence + monitoring + improving labs + adequate gestational age + appropriate fetal weight + favorable maternal stability. Standard expected positive trajectory with appropriate management.

nursesNote: 1230 — 32 y/o G1P0 K. Nguyen admitted to L&D antepartum room 4 at 34 wk + 2 d gestational age with SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA + HELLP SYNDROME (Mississippi class 2 — platelets 78k; complete HELLP with all 3 criteria — hemolysis + elevated LFTs + low platelets); referred from outpatient OB clinic at 1115 today after BP 168/108 + 174/112 + 172/110 across 3 readings + 4+ proteinuria + new severe HA × 12 hr + RUQ/epigastric pain × 6 hr + visual blurring/photopsia × 4 hr + 3.5 kg weight gain / 5 days. PMH no chronic HTN/DM/autoimmune/renal/thyroid; pregnancy was uncomplicated until 33 wk visit (BP upper-normal 132/84 — pattern of rising BP over 2 wk). FAMILY HX: mother had preeclampsia 1st pregnancy; maternal grandmother had eclampsia (seizures postpartum) — moderate-risk profile per ACOG. ASPIRIN 81 mg prophylaxis was NOT initiated at 12 wk despite family history (gap in care; will document for QI review). Spouse age 34 supportive + at bedside; maternal grandmother at bedside; insurance + multidisciplinary team engaged. ON L&D TRIAGE 1145: 2 large-bore IVs (16-gauge) placed; Foley for hourly I/O; labs sent — CBC = Hgb 9.8 (DOWN from 12.4 at 32 wk = hemolysis), HCT 28, platelets 78,000 (HELLP <100k); SMEAR = schistocytes present (hemolysis); LDH 720 (≥600 = hemolysis); T bili 1.6 (≥1.2 supports hemolysis); haptoglobin <30 (low — hemolysis); AST 145 + ALT 168 (HELLP ≥2× ULN ~40); Cr 1.0 normal (rules out renal predominance of aHUS); UA 4+ protein + UPCR 1.8 significantly proteinuric; PT/PTT normal + FIBRINOGEN 380 preserved (rules out DIC + AFLP); GLUCOSE 92 normal (rules out AFLP hypoglycemia); AMMONIA 36 normal (rules out AFLP); ADAMTS13 sent (will help rule out TTP — clinically unlikely given preserved coags + RUQ pain + classic preeclampsia presentation); type & crossmatch 4 units PRBC + 2 units FFP + 1 platelet pack held; REFLEXES 3+ bilateral patellar without clonus; LUNGS clear (no pulmonary edema); cervical exam = closed posterior firm 0 station; FHR 142 baseline reactive on NST; EFW ~2200 g (50th %ile). HELLP DIFFERENTIAL ruled out — coags + fibrinogen + ammonia + glucose all consistent with HELLP over AFLP/TTP/aHUS. MAGNESIUM SULFATE LOADING + maintenance initiated 1215 per protocol: LOADING DOSE 4 g IV over 15 min via dedicated infusion pump with high-alert med double-check + barcode scanning + co-RN cross-check; MAINTENANCE 2 g/hr continuous IV infusion + dedicated line not co-infused with calcium-containing fluids (precipitates); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV antidote × 4 vials in clearly labeled bedside med drawer; Q1H TOXICITY surveillance — patellar DTR 2+ bilateral (normal — loss = early toxicity at Mg 8–10), RR 18/min (>12 acceptable — <12 = severe toxicity at Mg 10–13), UOP 45 mL/hr (>30 acceptable — magnesium renally cleared so <30 = accumulation risk), mental status alert + oriented + cooperative, lung sounds clear, gag reflex intact; magnesium level at 4 hr = 5.6 mEq/L (within therapeutic 4–7 mEq/L = 4.8–8.4 mg/dL); SEVERE-RANGE BP treatment within 60 minutes per ACOG: LABETALOL 20 mg IV at 1200 → BP 152/98 at 1215 (improved from 174/112); labetalol 40 mg IV at 1430 → BP 138/86 at 1445; transitioned to oral labetalol 200 mg PO BID + nifedipine ER 30 mg PO daily after stable × 12 hr; goal SBP <160 + DBP <110 maintained throughout; ANTENATAL BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM administered 1230 (dose 1 of 2; dose 2 in 24 hr = 1230 next day) for fetal lung maturity (late preterm rescue 34+ eligible since not received in this pregnancy + likely delivery within 7 days); maternal glucose monitoring instituted (steroid-induced hyperglycemia anticipated — fingersticks q4h; will use insulin sliding scale if BG >180); FETAL surveillance: continuous EFM with baseline 142 + reduced variability (5–10 bpm — EXPECTED on magnesium; do NOT mistake for fetal compromise) + no decelerations + intermittent accelerations limited; NST done before magnesium was reactive; BPP 8/8 12 hr ago; FHR will be monitored continuously; magnesium expected to affect variability + reactivity but no concerning decelerations or bradycardia. MULTIDISCIPLINARY: MFM perinatology consulted + bedside rounds; OB hospitalist primary; ANESTHESIA bedside + epidural placement considered when platelet count + clinical status permit (platelets 78k currently borderline — anesthesia discussing platelet transfusion threshold + neuraxial vs general anesthesia plan if cesarean needed); NICU level II/III notified + neonatology aware + resuscitation team will be at delivery; LACTATION consult activated for breastfeeding planning; SOCIAL WORK + CHAPLAIN available; family + spouse + grandmother engaged. DELIVERY PLAN discussed with patient + spouse + family + medical team: expectant management × 24 hr post-second betamethasone dose IF maternal-fetal stable (target induction ~Day 2 evening or Day 3 morning); cesarean reserved for maternal deterioration + non-reassuring fetal status + obstetric indication; FAVORED VAGINAL delivery initially given cervix amenable to ripening + EFW 2200 g appropriate + stable maternal status; foley cervical ripening + oxytocin induction approach planned; epidural anesthesia preferred over general (avoid airway management + maternal instability + neonatal effects) IF platelets adequate (will reassess at delivery time; institutional cutoff ~70k for neuraxial varies); if cesarean → magnesium continues through delivery (eclampsia prophylaxis); uterotonics — OXYTOCIN first-line, AVOID methylergonovine (contraindicated in HTN) + AVOID carboprost (contraindicated in asthma — patient has no asthma but verify; carboprost can also cause flushing + diarrhea + bronchospasm), misoprostol acceptable; blood products available; ICU-level monitoring post-cesarean × 24 hr; magnesium continued × 24 hr postpartum routinely + extended if persistent severe sx. CONTINUOUS MONITORING orders: q1h vital signs + magnesium toxicity assessment (DTR + RR + UOP + mental status + lungs) + cardiac telemetry + continuous EFM + I/O hourly + cervical assessment q2–4h; LABS q6h CBC + LFTs + Cr + LDH + uric acid + magnesium level; ECHO if SBP not responsive to standard or if pulmonary edema concern; HEAD CT if focal neuro signs (rule out stroke); EEG if seizure activity. FLUID RESTRICTION 80 mL/hr TOTAL (including magnesium maintenance) — preeclampsia + capillary leak + risk of pulmonary edema; AVOID overaggressive fluid; D5LR or LR at restricted rate; magnesium sulfate in NS at 2 g/hr counts toward total. PATIENT + FAMILY EDUCATION provided: HELLP diagnosis + magnesium purpose + side effects (warmth + flushing + sedation common; severe = stop infusion + tell RN) + BP goals + betamethasone timing + delivery plan + neonatal NICU expectations + postpartum vigilance × 24–48 hr + later 6 weeks + eclampsia warning signs (return ER if HA + visual changes + RUQ pain + edema + SOB postpartum) + breastfeeding planning + aspirin prophylaxis in subsequent pregnancy + recurrence risk + long-term cardiovascular risk + lifestyle + screening + 6-week postpartum visit + signs of postpartum mood disorders. EMOTIONAL SUPPORT: chaplain visit offered + accepted (patient + family identify as Buddhist + Catholic blend; comfort sought from both traditions); social work assessment; family information sharing per patient consent. SPOUSE + MATERNAL GRANDMOTHER at bedside throughout; spouse approved emergency consent for surgery + transfusion if needed (patient is decisional currently); information sharing per patient consent. PLAN: continue magnesium + IV antihypertensives transitioning to PO + betamethasone × 2 doses + multidisciplinary management; q1h magnesium toxicity surveillance; q4–6h labs trending; expectant × 24 hr post-second betamethasone IF stable; expedited delivery if maternal deterioration or fetal compromise; cesarean if obstetric indication or non-reassuring fetal; epidural if platelets adequate + clinical status permits; postpartum magnesium × 24 hr + BP monitoring + outpatient transition; neonate to NICU; family + multidisciplinary engagement throughout. 1500 UPDATE (4 hr into magnesium maintenance + after second labetalol dose): patient stable, BP 138/86 MAP 103 on labetalol IV + ongoing magnesium 2 g/hr, reflexes 2+, UOP 50 mL/hr × past 2 hr, magnesium level 5.6 mEq/L within therapeutic, RUQ pain 5 → 2/10 + HA 5 → 2/10 (improving — reassuring), visual sx improved, no new neuro sx, fetal HR 138 with reduced variability (expected on magnesium) + no decelerations + intermittent accelerations limited; patient resting + family at bedside; no eclampsia + no pulmonary edema + no abruption + no fetal compromise.
providerOrders: ADMIT to L&D antepartum room 4 (single-room with continuous CTG + arterial line + central monitoring + bedside calcium gluconate antidote)

DIAGNOSIS: severe preeclampsia with HELLP syndrome (Mississippi class 2 — complete HELLP with hemolysis + elevated LFTs + platelets 78,000) at 34 wk + 2 d G1P0; rule-outs: AFLP (glucose + fibrinogen + ammonia normal — RULED OUT), TTP (no neuro/fever; ADAMTS13 pending — likely RULED OUT), aHUS (renal function normal — RULED OUT)
CODE STATUS: FULL CODE; HCP = spouse
DRUG SAFETY (HIGH-ALERT magnesium sulfate + IV antihypertensives + uterotonics):
Barcode scanning + co-RN cross-check + smart pump hard limits for magnesium
CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV antidote × 4 vials at bedside (clearly labeled drawer)
Dedicated IV line for magnesium (no calcium-containing fluids on same line — precipitates)
Standardized order set per L&D severe preeclampsia/HELLP protocol
MAGNESIUM SULFATE:
LOADING DOSE 4 g IV in 100 mL D5W over 15–20 min via dedicated infusion pump (NEVER bolus IV push)
MAINTENANCE 2 g/hr continuous IV infusion (in NS at 50–100 mL/hr concentration per institutional protocol)
Q1H toxicity surveillance: DTR (patellar) + RR (full 60 sec) + UOP + mental status + lungs

HOLD magnesium + administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV SLOW over 3 min if: loss of patellar reflex OR RR <12/min OR UOP <30 mL/hr (over preceding 4 hr) OR altered mental status OR magnesium level >8 mEq/L
Magnesium level at 4 hr post-loading + q6h thereafter; target 4–7 mEq/L (4.8–8.4 mg/dL)
Continue magnesium × 24 hr postpartum routinely; extend if persistent severe symptoms
AVOID concurrent calcium-containing IV fluids (precipitates); AVOID concurrent neuromuscular blockers (potentiates) if anesthesia involved
SEVERE-RANGE BP TREATMENT (≥160 SBP or ≥110 DBP sustained ≥15 min) — TREAT WITHIN 60 MINUTES per ACOG:
FIRST-LINE: IV LABETALOL 20 mg → repeat 40 mg at 10 min → repeat 80 mg at 10 min q10 min as needed (max cumulative 220 mg per crisis)
ALTERNATIVE: IV HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg → repeat 5–10 mg at 20 min (max 30 mg)
ALTERNATIVE: PO IMMEDIATE-RELEASE NIFEDIPINE 10 mg → repeat 20 mg at 20 min (max 50 mg in 1 hr)
Target SBP <160 + DBP <110 (NOT <140/90 acutely — too rapid drop risks placental hypoperfusion + ischemia)
Transition to PO antihypertensives when stable × 12 hr: labetalol PO 100–800 mg BID; nifedipine ER 30–120 mg daily; methyldopa 250–500 mg BID-TID (less common now)
AVOID: ACE-I + ARB (teratogenic), atenolol (IUGR), nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity), thiazide diuretics (volume depletion)
ANTENATAL CORTICOSTEROIDS (fetal lung maturity):
BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hr apart (DOSE 1 administered 1230 Day 0; DOSE 2 scheduled 1230 Day 1)
Alternative: DEXAMETHASONE 6 mg IM q12h × 4 doses (equivalent efficacy)
Late preterm rescue eligibility 34+0 to 36+6 if NOT received in current pregnancy + likely delivery within 7 days
Maternal hyperglycemia monitoring during steroid course (q4h fingersticks; insulin sliding scale if BG >180)
Onset 24 hr after first dose; peak 48 hr; optimal delivery window 24 hr to 7 days post-second dose
DELIVERY PLAN:
EXPECTANT × 24 hr post-second betamethasone dose IF maternal-fetal stable (target induction Day 2 evening or Day 3 morning)

EXPEDITED DELIVERY if maternal deterioration (worsening HELLP, eclampsia, abruption, refractory hypertension, pulmonary edema, severe RUQ pain, mental status change, vision changes, oliguria refractory)
EXPEDITED DELIVERY if fetal compromise (recurrent late decelerations, bradycardia, prolonged decelerations, abnormal BPP, growth restriction with abnormal Doppler)
VAGINAL delivery favored (cervix amenable to ripening + EFW appropriate + stable status)
Foley cervical ripening + oxytocin induction approach
Continuous EFM
Epidural anesthesia preferred (platelet count 78k borderline — anesthesia discussion + may need platelet transfusion to >70–75k pre-procedure if cesarean)

CESAREAN reserved for: unfavorable cervix not amenable to ripening + maternal-fetal deterioration not allowing time for induction + obstetric indication (malpresentation, prior cesarean, placenta previa, fetal distress not responsive)
Epidural anesthesia preferred if platelets adequate (institutional cutoff ~70k); general anesthesia if neuraxial contraindicated
Magnesium continued through delivery
Uterotonics: OXYTOCIN first-line; methylergonovine CONTRAINDICATED in HTN/preeclampsia; carboprost contraindicated in asthma; misoprostol acceptable; blood products available
ICU-level monitoring postoperative × 24 hr
NICU + neonatal preparation:
NICU level II/III activated + neonatology aware + resuscitation team at delivery
Anticipate mild RDS managed with CPAP + surfactant if needed + caffeine for apnea of prematurity
Magnesium crosses placenta — anticipate hypotonia + respiratory depression in neonate; NICU team prepared with resuscitation + supportive care
Breastfeeding initiation per maternal status + lactation consultant + skin-to-skin when feasible + pump if neonate in NICU
FLUID MANAGEMENT:
TOTAL fluid restriction 80 mL/hr (preeclampsia + capillary leak + pulmonary edema risk)
LR or D5LR at restricted rate; magnesium maintenance counts toward total
Strict I/O hourly via Foley
Daily weights
Watch for pulmonary edema (SOB + crackles + decreased SpO2 + low UOP + increasing fluid balance)
MONITORING:
Q1H VS + magnesium toxicity assessment (DTR + RR + UOP + mental status + lungs) + cardiac telemetry + continuous EFM + I/O
Q4–6H CBC + CMP + LFTs + LDH + uric acid + magnesium level
Cervical assessment q2–4h or as indicated
ECHO if SBP not responsive or pulmonary edema concern
HEAD CT if focal neurologic signs (rule out stroke)
EEG if seizure activity
PERIPHERAL SMEAR + ADAMTS13 + complement (rule out TTP/aHUS — pending; differential rule-out)
Coags + fibrinogen daily; type & crossmatch active
POSTPARTUM management:
Continue magnesium × 24 hr postpartum (eclampsia risk persists 24–48 hr highest; up to 6 wk)
BP monitoring q1h × first 24 hr + q2–4h × 24 hr + q4–6h until stable
Postpartum antihypertensives transitioned to PO (labetalol, nifedipine ER, methyldopa as appropriate)
Daily labs (CBC + LFTs + LDH + Cr) until improving
Education on postpartum eclampsia warning signs + emergency return + 6-week follow-up
Breastfeeding supported + lactation consultant + skin-to-skin
Postpartum mood screening (Edinburgh at 2 weeks + 6 weeks) — preeclampsia survivors at higher PPD/anxiety risk
Cardiovascular risk counseling + lifestyle + screening referral to PCP for 6–12 mo cardiac risk assessment
Aspirin 81 mg daily prophylaxis recommendation for subsequent pregnancies (start 12–16 wk)
Recurrence risk 15–25% baseline + higher if early-onset/severe/HELLP
MULTIDISCIPLINARY:
MFM perinatology (consultation + bedside rounds)
OB hospitalist (primary)
Anesthesia (epidural planning + cesarean readiness)
NICU + neonatology (resuscitation team at delivery + level II/III)
Lactation consultant (breastfeeding planning)
Social work + chaplain (emotional + spiritual support)
Case management (insurance + discharge + follow-up)
PCP communication for postpartum cardiovascular risk + chronic HTN if persistent
PATIENT + FAMILY EDUCATION:
HELLP diagnosis + magnesium purpose + side effects + report severe sx
BP goals + medication purpose + side effects
Betamethasone purpose + timing
Delivery plan + timing + cesarean vs vaginal decision factors
Neonatal NICU expectations
Postpartum vigilance × 24–48 hr + later 6 weeks
Eclampsia warning signs (HA + visual changes + RUQ pain + edema + SOB) — return to ER immediately
Breastfeeding planning + compatibility with medications
Aspirin prophylaxis in subsequent pregnancy starting 12–16 wk

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this L&D admission?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA per ACOG 2020 = preeclampsia (new HTN ≥140/90 + proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction at ≥20 wk) PLUS ≥1 severe feature; THIS PATIENT has multiple severe features = sustained severe-range BP 168/108 + 174/112 + 172/110 + severe HA × 12 hr unresponsive to acetaminophen + RUQ/epigastric pain + visual blurring/photopsia + new edema 3.5 kg / 5 days + hyperreflexia 3+ + 4+ proteinuria + UPCR 1.8
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines diagnosis. Multiple severe features confirmed. Severe HA + visual changes + RUQ pain are classic + concerning for impending eclampsia or stroke. Sustained severe-range BP requires treatment within 60 min per ACOG. Recognition triggers urgent management cascade.

opts.text: HELLP SYNDROME confirmed (Mississippi class 2 — platelets 50–100k; complete HELLP with all 3 criteria) = HEMOLYSIS (schistocytes + LDH 720 ≥600 + T bili 1.6 ≥1.2 + haptoglobin <30) + ELEVATED LIVER (AST 145 + ALT 168 ≥2× ULN ~40) + LOW PLATELETS (78,000 <100k); rules out differential — PT/PTT normal + fibrinogen 380 preserved + glucose 92 + ammonia 36 normal = consistent with HELLP over AFLP + DIC + TTP/aHUS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic confirmation + differential. HELLP = severe-preeclampsia spectrum with hematologic + hepatic involvement. Mississippi classification stratifies severity. Differential RULE-OUT (AFLP + TTP + aHUS + DIC) critical because management differs significantly. Coags + fibrinogen + ammonia + glucose all support HELLP.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE seizure prophylaxis initiated: 4 g IV loading over 15 min via dedicated pump + 2 g/hr maintenance; q1h surveillance — DTR + RR + UOP + mental status; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV × 4 vials at bedside as antidote; magnesium level 5.6 mEq/L therapeutic; loss of DTR or RR <12 or UOP <30 mL/hr triggers HOLD + calcium gluconate IV slow over 3 min + escalation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-alert medication administration + toxicity surveillance + antidote readiness. Magnesium reduces eclamptic seizure ~50% per Magpie. Q1H DTR + RR + UOP + mental + level monitoring. Calcium gluconate antidote at bedside. RN role: barcode scan + double-check + dedicated pump + q1h surveillance + immediate escalation if toxicity.

opts.text: SEVERE-RANGE BP TREATMENT within 60 minutes per ACOG: IV labetalol 20 mg → 40 mg at 1430 = improved 174/112 → 152/98 → 138/86; transitioned to PO labetalol BID + nifedipine ER daily after stable × 12 hr; goal SBP <160 + DBP <110 (NOT <140/90 acutely — placental hypoperfusion + ischemia); AVOID ACE-I/ARB + atenolol + nitroprusside + thiazides in pregnancy
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — severe-range BP treatment is TIME-SENSITIVE per ACOG to prevent stroke (leading cause maternal mortality) + abruption + acute heart failure. First-line = labetalol IV → hydralazine IV → nifedipine PO IR. Target reduction gentle. Pregnancy-specific avoidances. RN role: rapid administration + repeat dosing + monitoring + escalation.

opts.text: ANTENATAL BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hr apart for fetal lung maturity at 34+2 (late preterm rescue eligible since not received in current pregnancy + likely delivery within 7 days); reduces neonatal RDS ~40% + IVH + NEC + mortality; onset 24 hr + peak 48 hr; maternal glucose monitoring (steroid-induced hyperglycemia anticipated)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fetal lung maturity intervention. Late preterm rescue 34+0–36+6 if not previously received + likely delivery within 7 days. Onset 24 hr + peak 48 hr. Maternal glucose monitoring during steroid course. Delivery timing balances steroid window vs maternal-fetal status.

opts.text: DELIVERY PLAN — definitive treatment for HELLP; EXPECTANT × 24 hr post-second betamethasone IF maternal-fetal stable (target induction Day 2 evening or Day 3 morning); EXPEDITED if maternal deterioration or fetal compromise; VAGINAL favored (cervix amenable + EFW appropriate + stable); foley cervical ripening + oxytocin induction; epidural anesthesia preferred if platelets adequate (institutional cutoff ~70k borderline at 78k); CESAREAN reserved for obstetric indication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — delivery is the only definitive treatment. Timing balances steroid window vs maternal-fetal status. Vaginal preferred when stable + cervix amenable. Epidural anesthesia preferred (vs general) if platelets adequate. Multidisciplinary coordination (MFM + anesthesia + NICU + lactation).

opts.text: FLUID MANAGEMENT — TOTAL restriction 80 mL/hr (preeclampsia + capillary leak + pulmonary edema risk; LR/D5LR + magnesium counts toward total); strict I/O hourly; daily weights; watch for pulmonary edema (SOB + crackles + low UOP + increasing fluid balance); CAREFUL but not over-restrictive (preserve perfusion)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fluid management is delicate balance. Capillary leak + risk of pulmonary edema → restriction 80 mL/hr. But over-restriction → poor perfusion + AKI. Strict I/O + daily weights + clinical assessment. RN role: monitor + escalate if pulmonary edema or AKI signs.

opts.text: FETAL surveillance: continuous EFM with baseline 142 + REDUCED VARIABILITY (5–10 bpm) which is EXPECTED on magnesium + no decelerations + intermittent accelerations limited by magnesium; NST/BPP done before magnesium; magnesium crosses placenta — anticipate neonatal hypotonia + respiratory depression at delivery; NICU prepared
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — fetal monitoring with magnesium context. REDUCED VARIABILITY is EXPECTED on magnesium — do NOT mistake for fetal compromise. Recurrent late decelerations + bradycardia + prolonged decelerations would indicate compromise. NICU prepared for neonatal magnesium effects.

opts.text: MULTIDISCIPLINARY TEAM: MFM perinatology + OB hospitalist (primary) + anesthesia (epidural planning + cesarean readiness) + NICU/neonatology (resuscitation team) + lactation + social work + chaplain + case management + PCP communication for long-term cardiovascular risk; family + spouse + grandmother at bedside engaged
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multidisciplinary coordination is essential for complex maternal-newborn case. Each discipline contributes. Family engaged. RN role: facilitate communication + coordinate care + document handoffs.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies. Document and proceed. No medication allergies in current regimen. Not the priority cue but important for safe medication management.

opts.text: Administer methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM postpartum as first-line uterotonic for prevention of postpartum hemorrhage in this patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) is CONTRAINDICATED in HTN + preeclampsia + chronic HTN because it can cause severe hypertension + stroke + myocardial infarction. OXYTOCIN is first-line uterotonic for PPH prevention + treatment (safe in HTN). Carboprost contraindicated in asthma. Misoprostol acceptable. Drug-disease interaction recognition is the high-yield NCLEX point — METHERGINE in HTN/preeclampsia is a high-yield contraindication.

opts.text: RN gives magnesium sulfate 4 g IV PUSH over 2 minutes as a faster loading dose to expedite seizure prophylaxis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. MAGNESIUM IS NEVER GIVEN AS IV PUSH. LOADING DOSE = 4–6 g IV INFUSED OVER 15–20 MIN via dedicated infusion pump. Rapid administration causes profound hypotension + cardiac arrest. SMART PUMP with hard limits programmed prevents this error. The slow controlled administration is non-negotiable. This is a HIGH-ALERT medication safety + high-yield NCLEX error pattern.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What pharmacology + obstetric + safety principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: SEVERE PREECLAMPSIA diagnostic criteria per ACOG 2020 = preeclampsia + ≥1 severe feature (BP ≥160/110 sustained × ≥4 hr or treated; PLT <100k; AST/ALT ≥2× ULN; new hepatic pain unresponsive to medication; Cr ≥1.1 or doubled; pulmonary edema; new visual or cerebral symptoms); HELLP = HEMOLYSIS + ELEVATED LIVER + LOW PLATELETS; Mississippi class 1 ≤50k = worst, class 2 50–100k, class 3 100–150k; Tennessee complete vs incomplete
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic criteria framework. Severe preeclampsia ≥1 severe feature. HELLP = 3 criteria. Mississippi classification stratifies. Recognition triggers urgent management cascade. RN role: identify cues + escalate.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE pharmacology + administration: NMDA antagonist + smooth muscle relaxant + vasodilator + decreased uterine activity; LOADING 4–6 g IV over 15–20 min via DEDICATED PUMP (NEVER push); MAINTENANCE 1–2 g/hr; reduces eclampsia ~50% (Magpie); also tocolytic + fetal neuroprotection <32 wk (BEAM/PreMag); crosses placenta (reduces fetal HR variability expected + neonatal hypotonia/respiratory depression possible)
opts.rat: CORRECT — magnesium pharmacology framework. Loading dose + maintenance + dedicated pump + crosses placenta + multiple mechanisms + clinical effects. RN role: appropriate administration + recognize expected effects + recognize toxicity.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM TOXICITY surveillance + antidote: Q1H DTR (loss = early at Mg 8–10) + RR (<12 severe at Mg 10–13) + UOP (<30 mL/hr renal accumulation risk; mag renally cleared) + mental status; Mg level therapeutic 4–7 mEq/L; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV SLOW over 3 min = ANTIDOTE; KEEP at bedside; HOLD mag + give Ca + assess + escalate if toxicity
opts.rat: CORRECT — toxicity surveillance framework + antidote. Q1H monitoring of DTR + RR + UOP + mental + levels. CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV slow over 3 min ANTIDOTE at bedside. Sequence: HOLD → Ca → assess → escalate → resume at lower rate if appropriate. HIGH-YIELD NCLEX. RN role: surveillance + immediate response.

opts.text: SEVERE-RANGE BP TREATMENT within 60 min per ACOG: FIRST-LINE = IV LABETALOL 20→40→80 mg q10 min (max 220 mg cumulative) OR IV HYDRALAZINE 5–10 mg q20 min (max 30) OR PO IR NIFEDIPINE 10 mg → 20 → 20 (max 50 mg in 1 hr); target SBP <160 + DBP <110 (NOT <140/90 acutely); AVOID in pregnancy: ACE-I/ARB (teratogenic), atenolol (IUGR), nitroprusside (cyanide), thiazides (volume depletion)
opts.rat: CORRECT — severe-range BP treatment framework. Time-sensitive (within 60 min). First-line agents + dosing + max. Target gentle reduction. Pregnancy-specific avoidances. RN role: rapid administration + monitoring + repeat as needed.

opts.text: ANTENATAL BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hr apart for fetal lung maturity 24–33+6 wk OR late preterm rescue 34+0–36+6 if NOT previously received + likely delivery within 7 days; reduces RDS ~40% + IVH + NEC + mortality; onset 24 hr + peak 48 hr; alternative DEXAMETHASONE 6 mg IM q12h × 4 (equivalent); maternal hyperglycemia anticipated (steroid effect — monitor glucose)
opts.rat: CORRECT — antenatal corticosteroid framework. Eligibility + dosing + alternative + effects + maternal monitoring. RN role: administer + document + maternal glucose monitoring + coordinate with delivery planning.

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL of MAHA + thrombocytopenia in pregnancy: HELLP (preserved coags + normal ammonia + normal glucose + RUQ pain + classic preeclampsia features); AFLP (HYPOGLYCEMIA + LOW fibrinogen + elevated ammonia + N/V + altered mental status + Swansea criteria); TTP (NEUROLOGIC + fever + ADAMTS13 <10% + PLASMA EXCHANGE first-line + do NOT transfuse platelets); aHUS (renal predominance + ADAMTS13 normal + complement + eculizumab)
opts.rat: CORRECT — differential framework. Each diagnosis has distinguishing features + different treatment. Wrong diagnosis = wrong treatment. Workup includes ADAMTS13 + complement + coags + glucose + ammonia + smear + LFTs + Cr.

opts.text: POSTPARTUM management: magnesium × 24 hr postpartum (eclampsia risk persists 24–48 hr highest, up to 6 wk); BP monitoring; postpartum antihypertensives PO; daily labs until improving; education on postpartum eclampsia warning signs + return ER + 6-week follow-up; aspirin 81 mg subsequent pregnancy 12–16 wk; recurrence risk 15–25% baseline + higher if early-onset/severe/HELLP; LONG-TERM cardiovascular risk 2–4×
opts.rat: CORRECT — postpartum management framework. Magnesium × 24 hr postpartum + ongoing monitoring + outpatient transition + aspirin prophylaxis subsequent pregnancy + long-term cardiovascular risk counseling. Education + follow-up. RN role: monitoring + education + coordination.

opts.text: Administer methylergonovine (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM as first-line uterotonic for active management of third stage of labor or postpartum hemorrhage in this patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) is CONTRAINDICATED in HTN + preeclampsia + chronic HTN because it can cause severe hypertension + stroke + MI. OXYTOCIN is first-line in HTN/preeclampsia. AVOID Methergine. Drug-disease interaction recognition. CARBOPROST contraindicated in asthma. Know the medication-condition contraindications.

opts.text: Target SBP <120 + DBP <80 (normal BP range) for this patient with severe-range BP — aggressive normalization will protect against stroke + abruption
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — too rapid drop. TARGET = SBP <160 + DBP <110 (severe-range avoidance) — NOT normal BP range. Aggressive reduction to <140/90 acutely → placental hypoperfusion + ischemia + abruption + fetal distress + decreased cerebral perfusion. GENTLE reduction over hours. Maintain BP in target range. Post-stabilization gradual transition.

opts.text: Transfuse platelets to >100k pre-delivery in this patient with platelets at 78,000 regardless of delivery route to optimize hemostasis
opts.rat: ⚠️ NOT ROUTINELY indicated. Platelet transfusion in HELLP is reserved for: significant bleeding + planned procedure with cutoff <70k (neuraxial anesthesia) + cesarean delivery with platelets <50k typically + active hemorrhage. THIS PATIENT at 78k — vaginal delivery does not require platelet correction; epidural anesthesia consideration with anesthesia team (institutional cutoff varies 70–80k). Prophylactic transfusion at 78k for hemostasis is NOT routinely indicated + introduces transfusion risks (TRALI, TACO, alloimmunization). Reassess at delivery + per anesthesia.

opts.text: RN should suspend EFM monitoring because magnesium reduces fetal HR variability and this would falsely indicate fetal compromise + create unnecessary intervention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. EFM continues continuously during magnesium + severe preeclampsia/HELLP management. The REDUCED variability is EXPECTED + RECOGNIZED — do NOT mistake for fetal compromise. EFM monitors for concerning patterns (recurrent late decelerations, prolonged decelerations, bradycardia, abnormal patterns) that DO indicate compromise + warrant action. Stopping EFM removes safety surveillance. Recognize expected magnesium effects vs concerning patterns.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this L&D admission
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Eclampsia Prevention + Magnesium Sulfate Toxicity Surveillance r/t severe preeclampsia/HELLP at 34+2 wk; magnesium 4 g IV load + 2 g/hr maintenance via dedicated pump; q1h DTR + RR + UOP + mental status; magnesium level q6h; CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV antidote at bedside; HIGH-ALERT med protocols
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Eclampsia prevention + safe magnesium administration are immediate. Magnesium prevents seizures (eclampsia is a maternal mortality cause). Toxicity surveillance is q1h + immediate response if signs. HIGH-ALERT medication. Foundation for safe management.

opts.text: Severe-Range BP Treatment + Stroke Prevention r/t sustained severe-range BP 168/108–174/112; IV labetalol within 60 min per ACOG → improved to 138/86; transitioned to PO labetalol + nifedipine ER after stable; goal SBP <160 + DBP <110; AVOID Methergine + ACE-I/ARB + atenolol + nitroprusside + thiazides
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Stroke is leading cause maternal mortality in preeclampsia. Severe-range BP treatment within 60 min per ACOG critical. Gentle reduction. Avoidance of contraindicated agents (Methergine in HTN especially).

opts.text: Delivery Planning + Multidisciplinary Coordination r/t delivery is only definitive treatment; expectant × 24 hr post-second betamethasone IF stable; expedited if deterioration; vaginal favored with foley + oxytocin; cesarean reserved for indications; epidural preferred if platelets adequate; MFM + OB + anesthesia + NICU + lactation
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Delivery is definitive. Timing balances steroid window vs maternal-fetal status. Vaginal vs cesarean clinical decision. Multidisciplinary coordination essential. NICU prepared for late preterm + magnesium effects.

opts.text: Fetal Lung Maturity + Neonatal Preparation r/t late preterm rescue betamethasone 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hr apart; NICU level II/III prepared for mild RDS + magnesium effects (hypotonia + respiratory depression); resuscitation team at delivery; breastfeeding planning + lactation; maternal glucose monitoring during steroid course
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Betamethasone reduces RDS + IVH + NEC + mortality. NICU prepared. Maternal glucose monitoring. Breastfeeding planning. Foundation for neonatal outcome.

opts.text: Patient + Family Education + Postpartum Follow-Up r/t HELLP diagnosis + plan + magnesium + BP medications + betamethasone + delivery + neonatal NICU + postpartum vigilance × 24–48 hr + 6-week follow-up + aspirin prophylaxis subsequent pregnancy + recurrence risk + long-term cardiovascular risk + lifestyle + screening + postpartum mood
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + at discharge. Comprehensive education prepares patient + family for ongoing care + future pregnancies + cardiovascular health. Foundation for safe transition + long-term health.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV LOADING DOSE OVER 15 MIN via dedicated infusion pump (NEVER bolus IV push) + 2 g/hr maintenance continuous IV infusion; q1h DTR + RR + UOP + mental status; magnesium level at 4 hr then q6h (target 4–7 mEq/L); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV antidote at bedside; HIGH-ALERT med protocols with barcode + double-check + smart pump hard limits
opts.rat: Eclampsia prevention is essential. Magnesium administration with high-alert med protocols. Toxicity surveillance + antidote at bedside. Standard care.

opts.text: IV LABETALOL 20 mg → 40 mg → 80 mg q10 min as needed (max cumulative 220 mg) for severe-range BP within 60 min per ACOG; target SBP <160 + DBP <110 (NOT <140/90 acutely); transitioned to PO labetalol + nifedipine ER after stable
opts.rat: Severe-range BP treatment within 60 min per ACOG to prevent stroke + abruption + acute heart failure. Labetalol first-line + alternatives hydralazine + nifedipine. Target gentle reduction. Standard care.

opts.text: ANTENATAL BETAMETHASONE 12 mg IM × 2 doses 24 hr apart for fetal lung maturity (late preterm rescue 34+2 wk eligible — not previously received + likely delivery within 7 days); maternal glucose monitoring; NICU prepared
opts.rat: Fetal lung maturity reduces RDS + IVH + NEC + mortality. Late preterm rescue eligibility met. Standard care.

opts.text: FLUID RESTRICTION 80 mL/hr TOTAL (LR/D5LR + magnesium counts toward total); strict I/O hourly; daily weights; watch for pulmonary edema; CAREFUL but not over-restrictive (preserve perfusion + UOP >30 mL/hr)
opts.rat: Capillary leak + pulmonary edema risk in preeclampsia → fluid restriction. Strict I/O. Daily weights. Pulmonary edema surveillance. Standard care.

opts.text: DELIVERY PLAN: expectant × 24 hr post-second betamethasone IF maternal-fetal stable (target induction Day 2 evening or Day 3 morning); expedited if deterioration; vaginal favored with foley + oxytocin; cesarean reserved for indication; epidural anesthesia preferred if platelets adequate (78k borderline — institutional cutoff varies)
opts.rat: Delivery is only definitive treatment. Timing balances steroid window vs maternal-fetal status. Vaginal vs cesarean clinical decision. Standard care.

opts.text: MAGNESIUM SULFATE 4 g IV PUSH OVER 2 MIN as faster loading dose to expedite seizure prophylaxis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Magnesium is NEVER given as IV push. Loading dose is INFUSED over 15–20 MIN via dedicated infusion pump. Rapid administration causes profound hypotension + cardiac arrest. SMART PUMP with hard limits prevents this error. HIGH-ALERT medication safety + high-yield NCLEX error pattern.

opts.text: Administer METHYLERGONOVINE (Methergine) 0.2 mg IM as first-line uterotonic for prevention or treatment of postpartum hemorrhage in this patient
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Methylergonovine is CONTRAINDICATED in HTN + preeclampsia + chronic HTN — can cause severe hypertension + stroke + MI. OXYTOCIN is first-line uterotonic in HTN/preeclampsia. Misoprostol acceptable; carboprost contraindicated in asthma. Drug-disease interaction recognition is HIGH-YIELD NCLEX.

opts.text: Use IV LISINOPRIL or another ACE inhibitor for severe-range BP treatment given strong evidence for stroke prevention in adults
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. ACE INHIBITORS + ARBs are TERATOGENIC in pregnancy (renal/cranial anomalies + fetal demise late pregnancy) + CONTRAINDICATED. First-line antihypertensives in pregnancy = LABETALOL + HYDRALAZINE + NIFEDIPINE + methyldopa. AVOID ACE-I/ARB + atenolol + nitroprusside + thiazides.

opts.text: Target SBP <120 + DBP <80 for severe-range BP — aggressive normalization protects against stroke
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. TARGET = SBP <160 + DBP <110 (avoid severe range) — NOT normal BP. Aggressive reduction to <140/90 acutely → placental hypoperfusion + abruption + fetal distress + cerebral hypoperfusion. GENTLE reduction over hours. Maintain target range. Gradual outpatient transition.

opts.text: Transfuse platelets to >100k pre-delivery regardless of delivery route to optimize hemostasis in HELLP with platelets 78,000
opts.rat: NOT ROUTINELY indicated. Platelet transfusion in HELLP reserved for: significant bleeding + planned cesarean with platelets <50k + neuraxial anesthesia with platelets <70k (institutional variation) + active hemorrhage. THIS PATIENT at 78k — vaginal delivery doesn't require correction; epidural with anesthesia discussion may need transfusion. Prophylactic transfusion at 78k introduces transfusion risks (TRALI, TACO, alloimmunization).

opts.text: Suspend continuous EFM because magnesium reduces fetal HR variability and may create false concern + unnecessary intervention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. EFM continues continuously. Reduced variability is EXPECTED + RECOGNIZED on magnesium — do NOT mistake for compromise. EFM monitors for concerning patterns (recurrent late decelerations, prolonged decelerations, bradycardia) that DO indicate compromise + warrant action. Stopping EFM removes safety surveillance.

opts.text: ASPIRIN 81 mg daily prophylaxis for subsequent pregnancies starting 12–16 wk gestation through 36 wk per ACOG/USPSTF; counsel on recurrence risk + early surveillance + MFM + lifestyle + long-term cardiovascular risk
opts.rat: Standard subsequent-pregnancy preeclampsia prophylaxis. Class A recommendation. Reduces preeclampsia 24% in high-risk per ASPRE. RN role: counsel + educate + ensure follow-up + early surveillance referral.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at this L&D admission
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: ESTABLISH 2 large-bore IVs (16-gauge) + Foley + continuous CTG + cardiac telemetry; ADMINISTER magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading over 15 min via dedicated pump + 2 g/hr maintenance + calcium gluconate 1 g IV × 4 vials at bedside in clearly labeled drawer; SEND CBC + CMP + LFTs + LDH + uric acid + magnesium + coags + fibrinogen + glucose + ammonia + smear + ADAMTS13 + type & crossmatch
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate eclampsia prevention with magnesium + monitoring + diagnostic workup + differential rule-out. IV access + monitoring + medication + labs simultaneous. Foundation for all subsequent care.

opts.text: TREAT SEVERE-RANGE BP within 60 minutes per ACOG: IV labetalol 20 mg → repeat 40 mg at 10 min as needed → repeat 80 mg q10 min (max 220 mg cumulative); reassess BP at 10 min after each dose; target SBP <160 + DBP <110; transition to PO antihypertensives after stable × 12 hr
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Severe-range BP treatment is time-sensitive per ACOG (within 60 min) to prevent stroke. First-line agents. Concurrent with magnesium initiation. Reassess + repeat as needed.

opts.text: ADMINISTER betamethasone 12 mg IM dose 1 (dose 2 in 24 hr) for fetal lung maturity at 34+2 wk (late preterm rescue eligible); maternal glucose monitoring q4h fingersticks during steroid course; NICU notified
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Fetal lung maturity reduces RDS + IVH + NEC + mortality. Late preterm rescue eligible. Maternal glucose monitoring. NICU prepared.

opts.text: COORDINATE multidisciplinary team — MFM perinatology + OB hospitalist + anesthesia + NICU/neonatology + lactation + social work + chaplain + case management; family meeting on delivery plan + expectant × 24 hr post-second steroid + cesarean indication criteria + epidural anesthesia plan + NICU expectations + postpartum vigilance + long-term cardiovascular risk
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Multidisciplinary coordination essential for complex maternal-newborn case. Family meeting communicates plan + sets expectations. Each discipline contributes. Foundation for delivery + neonatal care.

opts.text: INITIATE Q1H magnesium toxicity surveillance (DTR + RR + UOP + mental status + lungs) + q4h cervical assessment + continuous EFM + I/O hourly + q6h labs + daily weights; fluid restriction 80 mL/hr total; q1h vital signs; comprehensive patient + family education on HELLP + magnesium + BP + steroids + delivery + NICU + postpartum vigilance + long-term care
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing + foundational. Continuous monitoring + education + family engagement throughout admission. RN role: surveillance + escalation + patient advocacy + comprehensive care.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1500 Day 0 (4 hr into magnesium maintenance + after labetalol 2nd dose): BP 138/86 MAP 103 on labetalol IV + magnesium 2 g/hr; reflexes 2+; UOP 50 mL/hr; magnesium level 5.6 mEq/L within therapeutic 4–7; RUQ pain 5 → 2/10 + HA 5 → 2/10 + visual sx improved; no new neuro sx; fetal HR 138 with reduced variability expected on magnesium + no decelerations + accelerations limited; patient resting + family at bedside
opts.rat: Standard expected response — symptoms improving with treatment + magnesium therapeutic + BP controlled + fetal status reassuring (expected effects of magnesium on variability). Standard expected trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1 morning 0700 (24 hr — second betamethasone dose): BP 132/82 MAP 99 on PO labetalol + nifedipine ER + magnesium continued; reflexes 2+; UOP 55 mL/hr; magnesium level 6.2 within therapeutic; betamethasone dose 2 administered 0700; HELLP labs stable to slightly improving (platelets 76k stable; AST 150 slight rise from 145; ALT 172 slight rise from 168; LDH 690 stable; T bili 1.4 down from 1.6); multidisciplinary rounds; delivery plan = induction Day 2 evening or Day 3 morning IF stable
opts.rat: Standard expected stable trajectory at 24 hr — BP controlled + symptoms resolved + labs stable to mildly improving + steroid window completed. Multidisciplinary rounds + delivery plan refined. Standard pre-delivery state.

opts.text: Day 1 1200: BP 152/96 → repeat IV labetalol 20 mg → 138/86 at 1215; reflexes 2+; UOP 45 mL/hr; magnesium level 6.0 within therapeutic; patient endorses mild HA reemerging + slight visual blurring; team alerted + neuro check repeated + reassuring; betamethasone glucose 195 → insulin sliding scale 4 units → BG 142 (improved); continued monitoring; no decelerations on EFM
opts.rat: Mild reemergence of preeclampsia symptoms + need for additional antihypertensive + steroid-induced hyperglycemia requiring insulin. Trends indicate possible disease progression — close monitoring + reassessment + escalation if continued worsening. RN role: recognize + escalate + manage.

opts.text: Day 2 0200 — UOP drops to 25 mL/hr × 2 hr in a row + reflexes diminished (1+ patellar) + RR 14 + magnesium level 9.2 mEq/L (above therapeutic); RN HOLDS magnesium infusion + administers calcium gluconate 1 g IV slow over 3 min + escalates to OB + magnesium level rechecked at 30 min = 7.8 (within therapeutic) + reflexes recovered to 2+ + UOP improving 35 mL/hr next 2 hr; magnesium restarted at 1.5 g/hr (reduced from 2)
opts.rat: ⚠️ MAGNESIUM TOXICITY RECOGNIZED — UOP <30 mL/hr (renal accumulation) + DTR diminished + level above therapeutic. RN held mag + administered calcium gluconate antidote + escalated. Standard textbook response. Reduced rate restart appropriate after recovery. RN bedside surveillance + immediate action critical. NCLEX high-yield.

opts.text: Day 2 morning — patient develops eclamptic seizure (generalized tonic-clonic × 90 sec) despite magnesium prophylaxis; left lateral position + airway management + supplemental O2 + check airway + bedside team activated + magnesium continued (already on) + additional 2 g IV bolus over 5 min for breakthrough seizure + neurology + MFM emergent delivery decision; postictal but recovering; EFM bradycardia → returned to baseline post-resuscitation; emergent cesarean activated
opts.rat: ECLAMPSIA = preeclampsia + new-onset seizure. Despite magnesium prophylaxis, 10–20% of patients can still seize (Magpie 50% reduction). RN response: left lateral position + airway + O2 + safety + additional magnesium 2 g bolus + emergent delivery activation. Acute emergency requiring escalation + delivery. Standard expected emergency response. Recovery to baseline post-resuscitation + emergent cesarean.

opts.text: Day 3 — vaginal delivery completed at 0800 of 1880 g female neonate after 14 hr of foley + oxytocin induction; APGAR 7/8; magnesium continued × 24 hr postpartum; lochia rubra moderate; fundus firm at U-2; perineum 2nd degree laceration repaired; epidural anesthesia successfully placed (platelets 82k); breastfeeding initiated; neonate to NICU level II for mild RDS on CPAP + caffeine for apnea of prematurity; mother + spouse + grandmother present
opts.rat: Successful vaginal delivery as planned. Standard expected positive outcome with appropriate multidisciplinary management. Neonate stable + appropriate NICU care. Standard postpartum + neonatal trajectory.

opts.text: Postpartum Day 1 0600 (24 hr post-delivery): BP 126/80 MAP 95 on PO labetalol + nifedipine ER; magnesium discontinued × 24 hr postpartum (off at 0730 today); reflexes 2+; UOP 70 mL/hr improving diuresis (mobilizing fluid); platelets recovering 110k; AST 92 + ALT 105 downtrending; LDH 480 downtrending; T bili 1.0 downtrending; HELLP RESOLVING; perineum healing; breastfeeding established + lactation supportive; neonate stable in NICU
opts.rat: Standard postpartum HELLP recovery — labs downtrending + platelets recovering + diuresis improving + BP controlled + magnesium discontinued + breastfeeding established. Standard expected positive postpartum trajectory.

opts.text: Postpartum Day 14 outpatient: BP 124/78 on PO labetalol + nifedipine ER (BP responsive to weaning trial in coming weeks); HELLP labs normalized (platelets 220k + AST 28 + ALT 32 + LDH 220 + T bili 0.7); Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale 4/30 (negative); breastfeeding well-established + neonate transitioned from NICU to home 9 days postpartum; education on aspirin 81 mg prophylaxis subsequent pregnancy + recurrence risk + long-term cardiovascular risk completed; PCP follow-up at 6 months for cardiac risk; comprehensive 6-week visit scheduled
opts.rat: Standard 2-week postpartum recovery + outpatient transition. HELLP fully resolved + BP controlled with planned weaning + EPDS negative + breastfeeding established + neonate home + comprehensive education + follow-up. Standard expected positive trajectory.

opts.text: RN administers METHYLERGONOVINE 0.2 mg IM postpartum as first-line uterotonic + patient develops BP 220/130 + severe HA + new visual changes within 30 min + escalated to ED-level management + IV labetalol + hydralazine + magnesium readministered + medical emergency declared + lessons-learned + medication safety alert + institutional protocol revision
opts.rat: ⚠️ METHYLERGONOVINE CONTRAINDICATED in HTN/preeclampsia — drug-disease interaction. Severe hypertensive crisis. RN advocacy + medication safety culture + institutional protocol revision are responses to identified safety event. Just culture + near-miss reporting + ISMP-style high-alert med protocols. HIGH-YIELD NCLEX drug-disease interaction error pattern.

opts.text: 1-year follow-up: cardiac risk assessment by PCP — BP 118/76 on no medications (weaned by 8 weeks); HbA1c 5.2; lipid panel WNL; lifestyle compliance + Mediterranean diet + regular exercise + weight stable; subsequent pregnancy planned + counseled on aspirin 81 mg prophylaxis starting 12–16 wk + early MFM + early prenatal care + recurrence risk; mother + neonate doing well; healthy 1-year-old; long-term cardiovascular surveillance continues
opts.rat: Excellent 1-year outcome — full BP recovery without medication + cardiac risk surveillance + lifestyle compliance + subsequent pregnancy planning + counseling. Standard expected positive long-term trajectory. Demonstrates value of comprehensive postpartum + long-term care for preeclampsia survivors.
